text: Electrochemical synthesis is an emerging strategy to prepare large quantity of high-value organonitrogen compounds using renewable energy and recycled waste. Herein, using the molecular boron-imidazolate cage BIF-29(Cu) which possesses isolated copper species as the electrocatalyst, the urea production through co-reduction of $CO_2$ and $NO_3^-$ is realized at the metal cathode. Surprisingly, though BIF-29(Cu) possesses single-atom catalytic centers, a urea Faradaic efficiency of 68.5% and a partial current density of 424 µA cm⁻² is achieved, outperforming most reported catalysts in urea production. Through combining the in situ surface-enhanced infrared absorption spectroscopy (SEIRAS), in situ X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) and density functional theory (DFT) calculations, the catalytic reaction pathway for the production of urea is revealed. The CO2 reduction and $NO_3^-$ reduction occur at different copper centers, producing hydroxylamine (NH2OH) and *CO through the independent reduction pathway, and the C-N bond is constructed at a single copper site in the follow-up reaction through the catalytic cascade pathway, thereby realizing the production of urea. Therefore, using discrete MOF catalyst with well-defined active sites can not only enhance the structural stability of the catalyst, but also realize efficient synthesis of complex compounds through multi-step catalytic reactions.
